name: Valkyries
content:
# Valkyries

Odin's warrior maidens who select fallen heroes from battlefields to bring to Valhalla. Divine shield-maidens who serve the Einherjar mead and decide the fates of warriors in combat.

## Abilities and Powers

- Brynhildr: The most famous Valkyrie, punished by Odin for disobeying him, placed in an enchanted sleep surrounded by fire until awakened by Sigurd
- Göndul: A war Valkyrie who decides battles
- Skögul: Another battle Valkyrie, companion of Göndul
- Hildr: "Battle" - leads the eternal battle of Hjadningavig
- Geirahöd: "Spear of Battle"

## Archetypal Significance

Valkyries guide the worthy dead to their afterlife destination - divine beings who choose fate in battle.

keyMyths.title: The Valkyries Choosing the Slain
keyMyths.description: Valkyries are most famously known for their role in selecting the bravest warriors who have fallen in battle. They ride across the battlefields, often described as armored figures on horseback, and choose those worthy to be taken to Valhalla, Odin's hall.

keyMyths.title: Serving Mead in Valhalla
keyMyths.description: In Valhalla, the Valkyries serve the chosen warriors, the Einherjar, with mead and food, catering to their needs as they prepare for Ragnarok. This role highlights their nurturing and supportive aspect beyond their warrior function.

keyMyths.title: Valkyries and Fate-Weaving
keyMyths.description: Some accounts suggest Valkyries are not solely limited to battlefields but also involved in weaving the threads of fate. They influence the outcome of battles and the lifespan of individuals, connecting them to broader cosmic forces.
keyMyths.source: Njal's Saga, Darraðarljóð (part of Njal's Saga)

keyMyths.title: The Valkyrie Brunhild's Disobedience and Punishment
keyMyths.description: Brunhild, a prominent Valkyrie, disobeys Odin's orders in battle, leading to her punishment of being stripped of her Valkyrie status and placed in a ring of fire, to be awakened only by a hero worthy of her. This myth explores themes of obedience, free will, and the consequences of challenging divine authority.

sources.title: The Valkyries: The Women of Norse Myth
sources.author: Britt-Mari Näsström

sources.title: Die Walküren: Ihre Historische und Mythologische Bedeutung
sources.author: Otto Höfler

extendedContent.title: Origins and Roles
extendedContent.content:
The Valkyries, whose name translates to 'choosers of the slain,' are prominent figures in Norse mythology, deeply intertwined with warfare, fate, and the afterlife. Their origins are complex, with some depicted as daughters of royalty, while others are presented as divine beings, often associated with Odin, the Allfather. Regardless of their specific lineage, their primary role is to select the most valiant warriors from the battlefield after they have fallen in combat. These chosen heroes, known as the Einherjar, are then escorted to Valhalla, Odin's grand hall in Asgard.

In Valhalla, the Einherjar prepare for Ragnarok, the prophesied end of the world, where they will fight alongside Odin and the other gods against the forces of chaos. The Valkyries play a crucial role in their preparation, serving them mead and attending to their needs, ensuring they are ready for the ultimate battle. This depiction highlights the Valkyries' role not just as choosers of the slain, but also as providers and caretakers of the chosen heroes, emphasizing their importance in the cosmic order.

Beyond their role in Valhalla, the Valkyries are also depicted as influencing the outcome of battles. Some sagas portray them as actively participating in combat, using their divine powers to sway the tides of war, granting victory to those they deem worthy. This aspect of their character further solidifies their connection to fate and destiny, highlighting their ability to not only choose who dies but also to influence who lives and prevails in the brutal world of Norse warfare. Their influence extends beyond the mortal realm, impacting the balance of power and shaping the destinies of both gods and men.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Depictions
extendedContent.content:
The Valkyries are typically depicted as beautiful and powerful warrior maidens, often clad in shining armor and wielding spears or swords. Their appearance is intended to evoke both awe and fear, reflecting their role as harbingers of death and protectors of the worthy. They are frequently portrayed riding winged horses, soaring across the sky to reach the battlefields, a visual representation of their swiftness and their connection to the divine realm. These winged steeds further emphasize their otherworldly nature and their ability to traverse the boundaries between the mortal and immortal realms.

Common artistic representations of Valkyries include scenes of them hovering over battlefields, observing the carnage and selecting the fallen heroes. Another recurring image is them welcoming the Einherjar to Valhalla, offering them mead and preparing them for their eternal afterlife. Runes and other Norse symbols are often associated with the Valkyries, further linking them to magic, divination, and the mysteries of fate. These symbols reinforce their connection to the deeper, often hidden, forces that govern the cosmos.

Furthermore, the Valkyries' attire and weaponry are often imbued with symbolic meaning. Their armor represents their strength and protection, while their spears and swords symbolize their power to choose and their connection to the art of war. The mead they serve in Valhalla is not merely a beverage; it represents wisdom, inspiration, and the divine favor bestowed upon the chosen warriors. Through these symbolic elements, the iconography of the Valkyries provides a rich and multifaceted understanding of their role in Norse mythology, highlighting their power, beauty, and connection to the spiritual realm.
